subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: sunlight warms Earth's land, air and water, and amount of exposure affects warming or cooling. Georgia S3P1b: investigate with thermometers and chart the effect of sunlight on various objects — the same core idea that sunlight exposure warms materials.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio names the sun as the principal source of energy that warms Earth; Georgia S3P1a asks questions to identify sources of heat energy, explicitly including sunlight. Overlap is clear but Georgia frames it within a broader heat-energy standard.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: water changes from liquid to solid and solid to liquid due to changing energy. Georgia S2P1c: construct an explanation that some changes in matter caused by heating or cooling can be reversed, with heating or freezing of water as a named example.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: water on Earth is present in many forms and can change between liquid and solid. Georgia S1E1b: identify forms of precipitation (rain, snow, sleet, hail) as either solid (ice) or liquid (water) — touches solid vs. liquid water but only in a weather context, not the change process.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: physical properties of water change between solid and liquid due to changing energy. Georgia S5P1b: argue from observations that physical changes in the state of water are due to temperature changes — same phenomenon, but Georgia adds a particle-motion depth beyond Ohio grade 1.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: properties of objects and materials change when exposed to conditions such as heating or cooling, and not all materials change the same way. Georgia S2P1c: explain that some changes in matter caused by heating or cooling are reversible and some are irreversible — the same core content.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio addresses properties of objects and materials and how they change; Georgia S2P1a has students describe and classify objects by physical properties (color, mass, texture, hardness). Overlaps on material properties but Georgia's element omits the change-under-conditions idea.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: objects move in ways such as straight, zigzag, circular and back and forth, and pushes or pulls affect motion. Georgia SKP2a: investigate the relationship between an object's attributes and its motion (straight, circular, back and forth, fast and slow) when a force such as a push or pull is applied.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: a push or pull is a force that can make an object move faster, slower or change direction. Georgia S2P2a: plan and carry out an investigation to demonstrate how pushing and pulling on an object affects its motion — same core force-and-motion content.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio states that a force can make an object go faster, slower or in a different direction; Georgia S2P2b has students design a device to change the speed or direction of an object — same concept applied through an engineering-design framing.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have basic needs met by obtaining materials, energy and water from the environment. Georgia S1L1b: compare and contrast the basic needs of plants (air, water, light, nutrients) and animals (air, water, food, shelter) — the same basic-needs core content.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: plants get energy from sunlight and animals get energy from plants and other animals. Georgia S4L1b: model the flow of energy through a food web/food chain beginning with sunlight — the same energy-flow idea, but at food-web depth well beyond Ohio grade 1.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: living things survive only in environments that meet their needs. Georgia S3L1c: use evidence to explain why some organisms can thrive in one habitat and not in another — the same survival-environment relationship, framed through Georgia habitats at a later grade.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things interact with their environments as they meet their needs, and resources must be available to meet those needs. Georgia S1L1c: design a solution to ensure that a plant or animal has all of its needs met — same needs-met-by-environment content with a design framing.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio notes that seasonal changes in the local environment directly impact the availability of resources. Georgia S1E1d: analyze data to identify seasonal patterns of change, including changes to the environment — overlaps on seasonal environmental change but not the resource-availability link.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: time can be divided into categories such as past, present and future. Georgia SSKH3f: correctly use the chronology terms past, present, and future — the same time-category vocabulary.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio includes dividing time into categories such as months of the year. Georgia SSKH3e: correctly use the time words day, week, month, year — the same calendar-based time categories.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the ways basic human needs are met have changed over time. Georgia SS1H1b: describe how everyday life of historical figures (food, clothing, homes, transportation, communication) is similar to and different from life today — same past-vs-present daily-needs comparison, framed through specific figures.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: maps can be used to locate and identify places. Georgia SS1G2: identify and locate the student's city, county, state, nation, and continent on a simple map or globe — the same map-use-to-locate-places skill.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: maps can be used to locate and identify places. Georgia SSKG2a: differentiate land and water features on simple maps and globes — overlapping map-reading skill, but limited to land/water features rather than locating places.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: places are distinctive because of physical characteristics (landforms, bodies of water) and human characteristics (structures). Georgia SS1G3c: identify and describe landforms (mountains, deserts, valleys, coasts) — covers the physical-characteristics half but not human characteristics.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: families interact with the physical environment differently in different times and places. Georgia SS2G2b: describe how historic figures and the Muscogee (Creek) and Cherokee adapted to and were influenced by their environments — the same people-environment interaction across times and places, anchored to specific groups.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: diverse cultural practices address basic human needs in various ways and may change over time. Georgia SSKG1: describe the diversity of American culture by explaining the customs and celebrations of various families and communities — same cultural-diversity core, without Ohio's change-over-time element.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: cultural practices address needs in various ways and may change over time. Georgia SS2H2a: compare and contrast Muscogee (Creek) and Cherokee cultures of the past to those of Georgians today — captures cultural practices changing over time, but only for two specific cultures.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: individuals have responsibility to take action toward common goals in homes, schools and communities and are accountable. Georgia SSKCG2: describe positive character traits of good citizens such as honesty, respect, and self-control — overlapping civic-disposition content, but traits rather than responsibility for common goals.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: rules exist in different settings and fairness should guide rules and consequences. Georgia SSKCG1a: explain how rules are made and why — same rules-and-their-purpose content, without Ohio's explicit fairness principle.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio addresses rules and the consequences for breaking them. Georgia SSKCG1b: explain why rules should be followed — same core content on the purpose of following rules.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: rules exist in different settings and should be guided by fairness. Georgia SS2CG1: define the concept of government and the need for rules and laws — clear overlap on why rules exist, framed at the government level.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: wants are unlimited and resources are limited, so individuals make choices. Georgia SS1E2: explain that scarcity is when unlimited wants are greater than limited resources — the same scarcity concept in nearly identical terms.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: individuals make choices because they cannot have everything they want. Georgia SSKE4: explain that people must make choices because they cannot have everything they want — essentially the same statement.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: people produce and consume goods and services in the community. Georgia SS1E3: describe how people are both producers and consumers — the same production/consumption core content.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: people produce and consume goods and services. Georgia SS1E1: identify goods that people make and services that people provide for each other — the same goods-and-services content from the production side.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: people trade to obtain goods and services they want. Georgia SS2E3: explain that people usually use money to obtain goods and services and how money makes trade easier than barter — same trade-for-goods-and-services idea with added money/barter depth.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: currency is used as a means of economic exchange. Georgia SSKE3b: identify that U.S. coins and dollar bills (paper money) are used as currency — the same currency-as-exchange concept.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: currency is used as a means of economic exchange. Georgia SS2E3: explain that people usually use money to obtain goods and services and that money makes trade easier than barter — same money-as-exchange concept with an added barter comparison.
